questions.scenario:
A payment provider calls a verification endpoint and waits for a response:

route: POST /webhooks/payment/verify
required response: 2xx within 2 seconds
bad response effect: provider retries the webhook

Which invocation shape fits best?

options.text: Synchronous invocation through an HTTP integration, because the caller waits for the result.
SQS event source, because queue-based Lambda always returns an HTTP response to the original caller.
Asynchronous EventBridge event, because the provider should not know whether verification passed.
ECS desired count scaling, because Lambda cannot be used for short HTTP handlers.

questions.explanation: The provider is waiting, so this is synchronous from the caller perspective. A synchronous handler can return success or failure directly. Queues and async events are better when the caller should not wait for the final work.

questions.scenario:
The orders API writes an order, then emits:

eventType: receipt.email.requested
orderId: ord_1042
idempotencyKey: receipt:ord_1042

The email provider is sometimes slow, but checkout should not wait for email delivery. Which trigger pattern is a good fit?

options.text: Send a small message to SQS and let a Lambda worker process it with retry-safe idempotency.
Keep the email provider call inside the checkout request until it succeeds.
Invoke the worker synchronously from the browser so the user can watch retries.
Store the message only in CloudWatch Logs and ask operators to replay it manually.

questions.explanation: SQS gives the side work a buffer outside the checkout path. The idempotency key matters because queue-based processing can retry and deliver the same message more than once. Keeping slow email inside checkout makes the user path more brittle.

questions.scenario:
Queue evidence for receipt jobs:

messageId: 8b6d4a25
orderId: ord_1042
ApproximateReceiveCount: 3
last worker log: provider accepted email, then function timed out before recording success

What should the worker design protect against?

options.text: Duplicate real-world effects, by checking and recording a stable idempotency key such as receipt:ord_1042.
Target group health checks, by making /health return 200 for the queue message.
Missing container ports, by changing the Lambda memory setting to 3000 MB.
EC2 disk pressure, by writing every receipt body to /var/log first.

questions.explanation: At-least-once delivery means the same job can be attempted again. If the first attempt sent the email but timed out before recording success, the retry must not send a second receipt. Idempotency turns repeated attempts into one business effect.

questions.scenario:
A retry review for Lambda workers lists these controls:

log orderId and messageId
use idempotencyKey receipt:ord_1042
send failed poison messages to a DLQ after retries
alert on age of oldest SQS message

Which controls help operate queue-based Lambda safely?

options.text: Log stable event identifiers so one message can be followed across attempts.
Use idempotency before doing side effects that users can notice.
Monitor queue age and DLQ movement so retries do not hide a stuck worker.
Disable errors by catching every exception and returning success.

questions.explanation: Retries are useful only when the worker is safe and observable. Stable IDs, idempotency, queue age, and DLQ signals help the team know what happened. Returning success after a failed side effect can drop work silently.

questions.scenario:
An EventBridge schedule starts a nightly export Lambda. The function logs:

exportId=exp_7732 status=start
ERROR AccessDenied s3:PutObject bucket=devpolaris-orders-exports
REPORT Duration: 842 ms Status: error

What owns the retry and follow-up concern here?

options.text: The asynchronous event flow and Lambda error handling configuration, plus the function execution role permission.
The original browser request, because every scheduled job has a waiting user request.
The ALB target group, because schedules are delivered through target health.
The ECS service desired count, because EventBridge retries by starting more Fargate tasks.

questions.explanation: A scheduled EventBridge invocation is event-shaped and no browser is waiting. The logs show an execution-role permission problem for the Lambda function. Retry and failure-destination behavior should be reviewed in the async path.
